Paul begins this chapter with a startling command: imitate God. Not Moses. Not a spiritual giant or apostle. God Himself. This is possible only because you are His beloved children, adopted into His family through Christ. The measure of this imitation is Christ's love—not sentimental or conditional, but the kind that gave Himself up entirely, becoming a fragrant offering poured out for us. Everything that follows in this chapter flows from this center. You cannot separate personal holiness from the cross. The purity Paul demands—the absence of sexual immorality, impurity, greed, obscenity—is not arbitrary rule-keeping. It is the natural shape of a life being conformed to the One who emptied Himself completely for your redemption. When you choose differently, you are choosing a different god, making yourself an idolater. That is why Paul speaks with such urgency: the wrath of God comes on those who persist in disobedience, and you must not be partakers with them.

You have been transferred from darkness into light. This is not a metaphor for mild moral improvement—it is resurrection language. You were dead; now you are alive. The light you now are is meant to illuminate, to expose, not to be hidden. Walk as a child of light, which means testing what pleases the Lord and refusing fellowship with the deeds of darkness. This requires more than private virtue; it requires courage. The darkness does not appreciate exposure, and neither will those who dwell in it. Yet here is the paradox of verse 13: everything exposed by the light becomes visible, becomes a light itself. Your willingness to step into truth invites others into truth. Wake up, the ancient hymn says, and Christ will shine on you. This is not inspiration to feel good about yourself. This is a summons to a radically reoriented life where your time, your words, your desires, your very sobriety are surrendered to the Holy Spirit's filling instead of wine's emptiness.

The submission and love Paul prescribes in marriage are not separate from the spiritual transformation he has been describing—they are its crucible. Wives submit to husbands as to Christ; husbands love wives as Christ loves the church, giving Himself up for her sanctification. Notice that both require death to self. The wife dies to the illusion that she has no one to submit to; the husband dies to the fantasy that he can love without sacrifice. The model is Christ's relationship to the church: He wages total war against every stain and wrinkle, pouring Himself out to make her holy and blameless. A husband's love for his wife is not measured by what he receives but by what he gives, by how he nourishes and cherishes her as his own body. This is marriage as mystery—not something to be mastered or understood from the outside, but entered into and lived. When you stand before your spouse, you stand before one who bears Christ's image. The way you love or lead that person is the way you are either honoring or dishonoring the Lord. That is the weight Paul places here. That is what makes faithfulness matter so much.
